coredata.dc:title: Epigallocatechin gallate-derived carbonized polymer dots: A multifunctional scavenger targeting Alzheimer's β-amyloid plaques
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Xiaoding
Liu, Wei
Dong, Xiaoyan
Sun, Yan
coredata.dc:description:
The design of high-efficiency scavengers targeting β-amyloid protein (Aβ) plaques in the progress of Alzheimer's disease (AD) has been recognized as an effective way to prevent and treat AD. Herein, epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG)-derived carbonized polymer dots (E-CPDs) were synthesized for the first time via a hydrothermal method using EGCG, an Aβ inhibitor, as one of the raw materials. The inhibitory efficiency and fluorescent property of E-CPDs were elegantly modulated by adjusting the molar ratio of EGCG to nitrogen-containing dopant, o-phenylenediamine (oPD), and 75E-CPDs fabricated with 75 mM EGCG and 50 mM oPD showed the highest inhibitory capability. The multifunctionality of 75E-CPDs on inhibition of Aβ fibrillization, Aβ fibrils disaggregation, amyloid fluorescent detection, and intracellular reactive oxygen species scavenging was demonstrated. 75E-CPDs inhibited the formation of β-sheet-rich Aβ aggregates, alleviated Aβ-induced cytotoxicity of cultured cells from 47% to 15%, and prolonged the lifespan of AD nematodes by scavenging in vivo amyloid plaques, demonstrating much higher performance than either EGCG or EGCG-free carbon dots. Notably, 75E-CPDs could rapidly disaggregate Aβ fibrils on “second” scale, faster than any other disaggregating agents. The aromatic structure as well as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups existing on 75E-CPDs surface, which would interact with Aβ species via hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and hydrophobic interactions, played critical roles in their inhibition and disaggregation capabilities. This work reveals that potent CDs can be fabricated by using an Aβ inhibitor as the precursor, providing a new perspective for the design of multifunctional scavengers targeting amyloid plaques.
Statement of significance Alzheimer's disease (AD) is one of the top ten causes of death worldwide and seriously threatens human health. Recently, carbon nanomaterials have attracted much attention because of their good biocompatibility and capability in modulating Aβ aggregation via multiple interactions. This work has for the first time fabricated epigallocatechin gallate-derived carbonized polymer dots (E-CPDs) and revealed the multifunctional potency of E-CPDs on alleviating the multifaced symptoms associated with β-amyloid protein (Aβ) fibrillization in the progression of AD. Notably, E-CPDs exhibited enhanced fluorescence emission upon binding to Aβ fibrils, possessing potential as Aβ fluorescent probes. It is believed that this work would open a new horizon in the design of multifunctional carbon nanomaterials as a potent amyloid scavenger for AD theranostics.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of 75E-CPDs. (a) TEM image. (b) Size distribution. (c) High-resolution TEM image. (d) XPS energy spectrum. (e) High-resolution C1s XPS spectrum. (f) High-resolution N1s XPS spectrum. (g) FTIR spectrum. (h) UV-Visible absorption spectrum. (i) 3D excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectrum.

Inhibition of Aβ40 aggregation by 75E-CPDs. (a) Effect of 75E-CPDs on Aβ40 aggregation measured by ThT fluorescence assay. (b) AFM images of Aβ40 incubated without or with different concentrations of 75E-CPDs for 120 h. Scale bar, 2 µm. (c) Surface coverage of Aβ40 incubated in the absence and presence of various inhibitors for 120 h. The concentration of inhibitors was 80 µg/mL. (d) Far-UV circular dichroism spectra of Aβ40 incubated with 75E-CPDs at 72 h. Aβ40 concentration was 25 µM for (a)-(d). (e) Viability of SH-SY5Y cells incubated with 75E-CPDs. (f) Detoxification effects of 75E-CPDs on Aβ40-induced SH-SY5Y cytotoxicity determined by MTT assay. The aged concentration of Aβ40 was 25 µM, and the final concentration of Aβ40 was 5 µM in cells. The cell viability treated with PBS buffer only was set to 100%. The data were presented as mean ± SD (n = 6). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, compared to the Aβ40-treated group. (g) FDA/PI double staining assays. FDA-positive cells present viable cells, while PI-positive cells present dead cells. Scale bar, 50 µm.

Disaggregation of mature Aβ40 fibrils by 75E-CPDs. (a) Disaggregation kinetics of Aβ40 fibrils. Aβ40 concentration was 25 µM. (b) Normalized ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ40 fibrils incubated with different CDs and EGCG for 4 h at 37°C. Aβ40 concentration was 25 µM. (c) AFM images of Aβ40 fibrils after coincubation with 75E-CPDs (2.5–80 µg/mL) for 4 h. Scale bar, 2 µm. (d) Fluorescence images of Aβ40 fibrils after coincubation with 75E-CPDs (2.5–80 µg/mL) for 4 h. Scale bar, 100 µm. (e) Fast disaggregation of Aβ40 fibrils by 75E-CPDs monitored by ThT fluorescence using a stopped-flow spectrometer during 50 s. Aβ40 concentration was 5 µM.

Fluorescence detection of Aβ40 aggregates by 75E-CPDs. (a) Fluorescent spectra of (black) 50 µg/mL and (red) 100 µg/mL 75E-CPDs. (b) Fluorescent spectra of 75E-CPDs upon mixing with different concentrations of Aβ40 aggregates. The concentration of 75E-CPDs was 50 µg/mL. Excitation wavelength, 370 nm. Emission wavelength, 450 nm. (c) Fluorescence images of Aβ40 incubated with or without 75E-CPDs after 72 h at 37°C. 25 µM Aβ40 with 1,6) 0; 2,7) 2.5; 3,8) 5; 4,9) 10 and 5,10) 20 µg/mL 75E-CPDs were observed by ThT and 75E-CPDs fluorescence, respectively. ThT fluorescence was recorded on excitation at 450–490 nm (Blue Channel); 75E-CPDs fluorescence was recorded on excitation at 330–380 nm (UV Channel). Scale bar, 100 µm.

ROS scavenging activity of 75E-CPDs in SH-SY5Y cells. (a) Fluorescent images of SH-SY5Y cells. The ROS-positive cells show green fluorescence. Scale bar, 50 µm. (b) The scavenging effect of 75E-CPDs toward intracellular ROS induced by Aβ40 using the DCFH-DA probe. The final concentration of Aβ40 was 5 µM in cells. The DCF fluorescent intensity of cells treated with PBS buffer only was set to 100%. The data were presented as mean ± SD (n = 6). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, compared to the Aβ40-treated group.

In vivo assays with C. elegans (N2 and CL2006). Fluorescence images of (a)-(b) CL2006 and (c)-(d) N2 nematodes co-stained with ThT and 75E-CPDs. Fluorescence images of (e) N2 and (f) CL2006 nematodes co-stained with ThT. The white arrows in the figure refer to the Aβ deposition in nematodes. (g)-(i) Fluorescent images of inhibiting Aβ deposits in CL2006 nematodes with 75E-CPDs. Scale bars, 20 µm. (j) Survival curves of inhibitors-treated CL2006/N2 strain. Strains were classified as paralyzed if they failed to respond to a touch-provoked movement. (k) The average daily number of eggs laid by N2 and CL2006 nematodes with or without 80 µg/mL of 75E-CPDs treatment. (l) The total number of eggs laid by N2 and CL2006 nematodes with or without 80 µg/mL of 75E-CPDs treatment. The data were presented as mean ± SD (n = 3). The eggs of 5 nematodes each group were counted. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, compared to the N2 group. #p < 0.05, ##p < 0.01, ###p < 0.001, compared to the CL2006 group.
(a) Schematic illustration of the multifunctionality of E-CPDs. (b) Schematic representation of the synthesis of E-CPDs by using EGCG and o-phenylenediamine.

head.ce:title: Epigallocatechin gallate-derived carbonized polymer dots: A multifunctional scavenger targeting Alzheimer's β-amyloid plaques

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design of high-efficiency scavengers targeting β-amyloid protein (Aβ) plaques in the progress of Alzheimer's disease (AD) has been recognized as an effective way to prevent and treat AD. Herein, epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG)-derived carbonized polymer dots (E-CPDs) were synthesized for the first time via a hydrothermal method using EGCG, an Aβ inhibitor, as one of the raw materials. The inhibitory efficiency and fluorescent property of E-CPDs were elegantly modulated by adjusting the molar ratio of EGCG to nitrogen-containing dopant, o-phenylenediamine (oPD), and 75E-CPDs fabricated with 75 mM EGCG and 50 mM oPD showed the highest inhibitory capability. The multifunctionality of 75E-CPDs on inhibition of Aβ fibrillization, Aβ fibrils disaggregation, amyloid fluorescent detection, and intracellular reactive oxygen species scavenging was demonstrated. 75E-CPDs inhibited the formation of β-sheet-rich Aβ aggregates, alleviated Aβ-induced cytotoxicity of cultured cells from 47% to 15%, and prolonged the lifespan of AD nematodes by scavenging in vivo amyloid plaques, demonstrating much higher performance than either EGCG or EGCG-free carbon dots. Notably, 75E-CPDs could rapidly disaggregate Aβ fibrils on “second” scale, faster than any other disaggregating agents. The aromatic structure as well as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups existing on 75E-CPDs surface, which would interact with Aβ species via hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and hydrophobic interactions, played critical roles in their inhibition and disaggregation capabilities. This work reveals that potent CDs can be fabricated by using an Aβ inhibitor as the precursor, providing a new perspective for the design of multifunctional scavengers targeting amyloid plaques.

Alzheimer's disease (AD) is one of the top ten causes of death worldwide and seriously threatens human health. Recently, carbon nanomaterials have attracted much attention because of their good biocompatibility and capability in modulating Aβ aggregation via multiple interactions. This work has for the first time fabricated epigallocatechin gallate-derived carbonized polymer dots (E-CPDs) and revealed the multifunctional potency of E-CPDs on alleviating the multifaced symptoms associated with β-amyloid protein (Aβ) fibrillization in the progression of AD. Notably, E-CPDs exhibited enhanced fluorescence emission upon binding to Aβ fibrils, possessing potential as Aβ fluorescent probes. It is believed that this work would open a new horizon in the design of multifunctional carbon nanomaterials as a potent amyloid scavenger for AD theranostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer's disease (AD), the most common form of dementia, is a progressive neurodegenerative disease that has affected more than 50 million people worldwide in 2020 [1]. The AD patients will experience memory loss, cognitive impairment, disorientation, and other clinical symptoms [2–4]. Currently, although the detailed pathogenic mechanisms of AD have not been elucidated, the accumulation and aggregation of β-amyloid protein (Aβ) in the brain are considered as the main causes of AD [5,6]. Aβ is the enzymatic cleavage product of amyloid precursor protein via β- and γ-secretases, and Aβ40 is the most abundant form in human cerebrospinal fluid [7]. In the brain of AD patients, Aβ monomers can spontaneously self-assemble into soluble oligomers and insoluble fibrils, which causes cytotoxicity, and oxidative stress, and gradually leads to neuronal synaptic dysfunction, ultimately resulting in neuron death and nervous system damage [8,9]. According to the Aβ cascade hypothesis, blocking Aβ fibrils formation or disrupting the existing β-sheet structures in Aβ plaques by design of potent Aβ scavengers is of great significance for the prevention and treatment of AD.

At present, a variety of nanomaterials have been reported as Aβ inhibitors, including carbon dots (CDs) [10], magnetic nanoparticles [11], gold nanoparticles [12], silica nanoparticles [13], polymer nanoparticles [14], and lipid-based nanoparticles [15]. Among them, CDs have emerged as a new kind of Aβ inhibitor due to the simple preparation process, easy functionalization, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and adjustable optical properties [16–19]. The conjugated π electrons of CDs can interact with aromatic amino acid residues of Aβ via π-π stacking interactions, which may modulate the on-pathway Aβ aggregation and cytotoxicity [20]. On the other hand, CDs have the potential for amyloid detection, showing enhanced fluorescent signals upon binding to Aβ species [21]. Therefore, CDs are promising materials for Aβ inhibition and imaging. However, to the best of our knowledge, most fluorescent CDs have been utilized for photodynamic therapy [22], light-emitting diodes fabrication [23], and detection probes preparation [24], and there are few reports of disaggregation and inhibition of the formation of Aβ aggregates, as well as amyloid imaging by CDs. Hence, CDs materials deserve extensive explorations for AD-related applications.

EGCG (>95%) was purchased from Meilunbio (Dalian, China). o-Phenylenediamine (oPD), bovine serum albumin (BSA), thioflavin T (ThT), 1,1,1,3,3,3-hexafluoro-2-propanol (HFIP), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Congo red (CR) was acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology (Shanghai, China). 1-Anilinonaphthalene-8-sulfonic (ANS) was received from TCI Development (Shanghai, China). Fluorescein diacetate (FDA) and propidium iodide (PI) were purchased from DingGuo Biotech (Beijing, China). Synthetic Aβ40, Aβ42, and hIAPP powder (>95%) were received from GL Biochem (Shanghai, China). Human neuroblastoma cells (SH-SY5Y) were obtained from the Cell Bank of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium/Ham's F-12 (DMEM/F12) were obtained from Invitrogen (Carlsbad, CA, USA). The wild-type N2 and transgenic CL2006 strains of Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans) were purchased from the Caenorhabditis Genetics Center (University of Minnesota, MN, USA). E. coli strain (OP50) was from Henan University (Kaifeng, China). All other reagents were obtained from local sources of the highest purity without further purification. Deionized water was used for all solution preparations.

E-CPDs were synthesized by a “one-step” hydrothermal method as described in Scheme 1 b. Briefly, oPD and EGCG were dissolved in deionized water (10 mL) at different molar ratios (1:0.04–1:2.25 and 0:75), respectively (Table S1). Then, the solution was sealed in a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave (25 mL) and placed in a 200°C furnace for 10 h hydrothermal treatment. After the reactor was cooled to room temperature, the E-CPDs solution was dialyzed against deionized water in a 500 Da dialysis bag for 48 h, and dialyzed against 50% ethanol for another 48 h. Then, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm nylon membrane to remove large particles. The solution was rotary evaporated to remove ethanol, followed by lyophilization to obtain E-CPDs powders, and stored at -20°C before use.

The morphology and size distribution of E-CPDs were characterized with a field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan). The elements and their chemical states of E-CPDs were analyzed with an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher, USA) with a mono X-ray source Al Kα excitation (1486.6 eV). The functional groups and chemical bonds of E-CPDs were detected with a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) (Nicolet 6700, Thermo Fisher, USA). The fluorescence spectra were recorded with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (LS55, PerkinElmer, USA). The ζ-potential was determined using a Zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern, UK). The UV-Vis absorption spectra were scanned with a microplate reader (TECAN Infinite, Salzburg, Austria).

Aβ40 monomer solutions were prepared as previously reported [34]. Briefly, a certain amount of Aβ40 powder was dissolved in HFIP to 1.0 mg/mL, set quietly at 4°C for 2 h. After the Aβ40 solution was clarified, it was sonicated in an ice bath for 20 min to disrupt the pre-existing amyloid aggregates. Then, the solution was freeze-dried with a vacuum freeze dryer (Labconco, MO, USA). The monomeric Aβ40 was immediately stored at -20°C.

Before to use, the lyophilized Aβ40 monomers were dissolved in 20 mM NaOH solution to 275 µM and briefly sonicated to facilitate dissolution. After centrifugation at 16000 g for 20 min at 4°C, the upper 75% of the supernatant was collected as Aβ40 stock solution. For inhibition of Aβ40 aggregation, the Aβ40 stock solution was diluted with PBS buffer (100 mM sodium phosphate, 10 mM NaCl, pH 7.4) containing various concentrations of E-CPDs to the final Aβ40 concentration of 25 µM. For disaggregation of Aβ40 fibrils, the mature Aβ40 fibrils could be obtained by incubation of 50 µM Aβ40 solution at 37°C for 5 d. Aβ42/hIAPP monomer solutions were prepared by the same procedure as described above.

ThT dye can specifically bind to β-sheet structures in Aβ40/Aβ42/hIAPP aggregates and show enhanced fluorescence intensity at 480 nm upon exciting at 440 nm. The fluorescence signal was recorded with the microplate reader mentioned above.

In aggregation kinetics assays, 200 µL samples containing 25 µM Aβ40/Aβ42/hIAPP monomers, 25 µM ThT, and different concentrations of E-CPDs were added to a 96-well plate and continuously incubated in the microplate reader mentioned above at 37°C. The fluorescence intensity was measured at 10 min reading intervals, and 5 s shaking before each read until the fibrillization reached the plateau phase. The final fluorescent results were obtained by subtracting the fluorescence intensity of the Aβ40-free solution. The kinetic curves were normalized and fitted to the following equation:

where y is the ThT fluorescence intensity at time t; y 0 and y max are the minimum and maximum ThT fluorescence intensity, respectively; t 1/2 is the time when the ThT fluorescence intensity reaches 50% of maximum fluorescence intensity; and k is the elongation rate constant. The lag time (T lag) was then calculated using the following equation [14]:
(2)

For measurement of the final ThT fluorescence of aggregation, the samples were placed in an air shaker (37°C, 150 rpm) and incubated for 5 d. Then, 200 µL of the samples were added into a quartz dish containing 2 mL of ThT solution (25 µM ThT, 100 mM PBS, pH 7.4), and the fluorescence intensity was detected with the fluorescence spectrophotometer described above. The data were the average of three independent experiments and represented as mean ± standard deviation (SD).

In disaggregation kinetic experiments, a solution of mature Aβ40 fibrils (prepared with 50 µM Aβ40 for 120 h) was added to PBS buffer containing different concentrations of E-CPDs with or without BSA to make the final Aβ40 concentration of 25 µM. Samples (200 µL in PBS buffer) containing 25 µM Aβ40 fibrils, 25 µM ThT, and different concentrations of E-CPDs and BSA were mixed in a 96-well plate and incubated at 37°C to detect the fluorescence changes.

The exposure of hydrophobic patches is a typical process during amyloid fibrillization. ANS is a fluorescent probe dye that can bind to the exposed hydrophobic surface of the protein, thus exhibiting enhanced fluorescence intensity and blue shift of the emission peak [35]. ANS solution (50 µM) was firstly prepared in ultrapure water. Then, 200 µL of Aβ40 (25 µM) sample with or without 75E-CPDs was mixed with 2 mL of ANS solution, and the fluorescence spectra upon 380 nm excitation were detected with the fluorescence spectrophotometer described above.

CR is often used to detect amyloid fibrils because of its high affinity for β-sheet structures. The dye solution of 50 µM was prepared in PBS buffer. Then, 100 µL of Aβ40 solution containing different concentrations of 75E-CPDs was mixed with an equal volume of the CR solution in a 96-well plate to keep the final concentration of Aβ40 at 25 µM. The absorption spectra from 400 to 600 nm were recorded using the microplate reader mentioned above.

Aβ40 solutions (25 µM) containing various concentrations of 75E-CPDs were incubated in an air shaker (37°C, 150 rpm) for 5 d. The samples (50 µL) were taken and deposited on ultrathin carbon films. Then, the samples were negatively stained with 50 µL of 2% (w/v) phosphotungstic acid for 3 min, followed by washing with ultrapure water. Each sample was observed using the transmission electron microscope mentioned above at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

In AFM observations, Aβ40/Aβ42 monomer solutions (25 µM) in the absence and presence of different concentrations of E-CPDs were incubated at 37°C for 120 h in the microplate reader mentioned above. The samples (50 µL) were then withdrawn and deposited on newly cleaved mica substrates and incubated for 5 min, followed by 5 times rinsing with ultrapure water to remove salts in the samples, and finally dried using a spin coater (KW-A4, IMECAS, China) at 1000 rpm for 60 s. The morphology was observed with a multimode AFM (CSPM5500, Benyuan, China) under tapping mode. The microfabricated silicon cantilever (Tap300Al-G, Benyuan, China) of 125 µm in length with a nominal spring force constant of 40 N/m was used. The cantilever resonance frequency was set at 300 kHz. As a control, different concentrations of 75E-CPDs sample solutions without Aβ were prepared in the same way as described above, and then each mica sheet was observed under the AFM.

In disaggregation experiments, mature fibril solutions of Aβ40 (25 µM) with different concentrations of E-CPDs were incubated at 37°C in an air shaker at 150 rpm. Samples were withdrawn at different time intervals (0, 5, 10, 15, 30, 60, and 240 min) and observed under the AFM. Each sample was observed in three different areas, and the selected area was representative of the three scans.

Circular dichroism spectroscopy was used to characterize the secondary structures of Aβ during aggregation. Samples obtained at 72 h were pipetted into a 1 mm path-length quartz cell. The ellipticity was recorded with a circular dichroism spectrometer (J-810, Jasco, Japan) in the spectral range 190–260 nm at a scanning speed of 100 nm/min, and a bandwidth of 1 nm. The data were the average of three consecutive scans.

Stopped-flow fluorescence apparatus (SX20, Applied Photophysics, UK) was used to investigate the rapid disaggregation phenomenon of Aβ40 fibrils by 75E-CPDs. Aβ40 fibril solutions (55 µM) containing 55 µM ThT were rapidly mixed with 1.1–11 µg/mL 75E-CPDs solutions in a volume ratio of 1:10 at 37°C. ThT fluorescence intensity at 480 nm was recorded with excitation at 440 nm.

In the aggregation fluorescence experiment, monomeric Aβ40 (25 µM) was incubated with different concentrations of 75E-CPDs (0, 2.5, 5, 10, and 20 µg/mL) at 37°C for 72 h. In the disaggregation experiments, Aβ40 fibrils were mixed with or without 75E-CPDs (2.5, 5, 10, 20, 40, and 80 µg/mL) in PBS buffer at 37°C for 4 h. The samples were observed with an inverted fluorescence microscope (TE2000-U, Nikon, Japan) at different intervals.

For fluorescent detection of Aβ fibrils, 100 µg/mL of 75E-CPDs solutions (in 100 mM PBS, pH 7.4, 3% ethanol) were mixed with different concentrations of Aβ40 mature fibrils solutions (0, 1.25, 2.5, 5, and 10 µM, aged for 5 d) in equal volume. The maximum emission fluorescence intensity of 75E-CPDs under the excitation of 370 nm was recorded with the fluorescence spectrophotometer described above.

MTT assay was performed to detect the cytotoxicity of 75E-CPDs and the inhibitory effects of 75E-CPDs on Aβ40-induced cytotoxicity. SH-SY5Y cells were cultured in DMEM/F12 medium supplemented with 10% FBS, 100 U/mL penicillin, and 100 U/mL streptomycin in a cell incubator at 37°C under 5% CO2. A total of 8 × 103 cells/well (80 µL) were added to a sterile 96-well plate and cultured for 24 h. Then, 20 µL of Aβ40 samples (25 µM) aged for 48 h with or without 75E-CPDs were added and incubated for an additional 24 h. After that, 10 µL MTT solutions (5.5 µg/mL in PBS buffer) were added to each well and cultured for 4 h. The media were removed by centrifuging at 1500 rpm for 10 min and replaced with 100 µL DMSO to dissolve the formazan crystals. After shaking at 37°C for 20 min, the absorbance at 570 nm was measured with the microplate reader mentioned above. The samples of each group were carried out with six replicates, and the average value was calculated. All the experimental values were obtained by subtracting the background signals of the media without cells. The cells treated with PBS buffer were set as control, and other groups were normalized with the control. The cell viability was calculated according to the following equation:
(3)
Cell
viability
( % )
=
100 %

( O

)

where OD Treated was obtained in the presence of 75E-CPDs.

The reduction of Aβ40-induced cytotoxicity by 75E-CPDs was detected by FDA/PI double staining assay. SH-SY5Y cells were pretreated with different concentrations of 75E-CPDs as described in Section 2.13. FDA/PI staining solutions were prepared in PBS buffer, where FDA was 10 µg/mL, and PI was 5 µg/mL (acetone assisted solubilization). After being cultured, the cell culture media were replaced with FDA/PI staining solutions and incubated at 37°C for 15 min. Then, the stained cells were washed with PBS buffer for 5 times and observed under the inverted fluorescence microscope mentioned above.

The scavenging capability of 75E-CPDs on Aβ40-induced intracellular ROS was detected by dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) assay with a ROS detection kit (Beyotime, S0033S). SH-SY5Y cells were pretreated with or without 75E-CPDs in the same way as they did in Section 2.13. After the cell culture media were removed, DCFH-DA was added, and the cells were incubated in dark for 20 min. Finally, the stained cells were washed for 3–5 times with PBS buffer, and the DCF fluorescence intensity at 525 nm was recorded under the excitation of 488 nm. At the same time, the fluorescence images of the cells were observed under the inverted fluorescence microscope described above. SH-SY5Y cells treated with PBS buffer only were set as the negative control.

C. elegans mutant CL2006 and wild-type N2 were used as in vivo models to explore the amyloid scavenging capability of 75E-CPDs. The nematodes were cultured at 20°C on a solid nematode growth medium (NGM) (3 g/L NaCl, 1 mM CaCl2, 1 mM MgSO4, 5 µg/mL cholesterol, 17 g/L agar, 2.5 g/L peptone, 250 mM KH2PO4, pH 6.0) and seeded with E. coli OP50 as food resources.

In the fluorescent detection of Aβ42 plaques in nematodes incubated with 75E-CPDs, wild-type N2 and mutant CL2006 nematodes at L4 larval stage were randomly selected and fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde at 4°C for 24 h. The fixed nematodes were washed with deionized water and stained with PBS buffer containing 80 µg/mL 75E-CPDs and 10 µM ThT for another 4 h. Finally, the nematodes were rinsed 3 times with deionized water, and observed with the inverted fluorescence microscope mentioned above.

For inhibiting Aβ42 deposits in CL2006 nematodes, 200 µL of E-CPDs (80 µg/mL) were added to an NGM medium plated with E. coli OP50. Wild-type (N2) and mutant (CL2006) C. elegans at L4 larval stage were transferred onto NGM plates, respectively. After incubation at 20°C for 3 d, the nematodes were collected and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde at 4°C for 24 h. The nematodes were then stained with 10 µM ThT for 4 h. The stained nematodes were washed 3 times with deionized water, and observed with the inverted fluorescence microscope mentioned above to evaluate the Aβ accumulation.

In the lifespan assay, an average of 60 N2 or CL2006 nematodes were transferred to fresh NGM plates containing E. coli OP50 with or without E-CPDs (80 µg/mL, 200 µL), respectively, and 300 µL of 150 µM fluorodeoxyuridine (FUDR) was added to the plates to inhibit the nematodes spawning. The number of surviving nematodes was recorded every day until all nematodes were completely dead. Each group of survivors was transferred to a new NGM plate containing E. coli OP50 and corresponding agents every 3 d. The nematodes were scored as dead when they failed to respond to a mechanical stimulus with a platinum wire [18].

The egg-laying experiment was carried out according to the previously reported methods with slight modifications [36–38]. After incubation with 80 µg/mL of 75E-CPDs for 24 h, five L4 nematodes were transferred to fresh NGM plates containing 75E-CPDs (80 µg/mL) and E. coli OP50. The number of eggs laid was recorded every 24 h, after which the nematodes were transferred to a new incubated NGM plate until no eggs were observed. The nematodes that were not incubated with 75E-CPDs served as the control group. The total number of eggs laid per nematode and the average number of eggs laid by each nematode per day were calculated, and all experiments were repeated three times independently.

All data were presented as mean values ± standard deviation (SD). All statistical analyses were conducted using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by a statistical comparison with the Tukey test, and p < 0.05 or less was considered to be statistically significant (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001).

As depicted in Scheme 1 b, E-CPDs were synthesized by a “one-pot” hydrothermal method using EGCG and oPD as the raw materials. Because the composition can influence the properties of prepared E-CPDs, different E-CPDs were synthesized by changing the molar ratio of EGCG:oPD from 0.04:1 to 0.1:1, 0.2:1, 1:1, 1.5:1, 2:1, and 2.25:1. The raw material compositions and corresponding names of different CDs are listed in Table S1. The morphology and structure characterizations were firstly performed by taking 75E-CPDs as an example, which were synthesized using 75 mM EGCG and 50 mM oPD. TEM image in Fig. 1
a shows that the shape of 75E-CPDs was quasi-spherical with an average size of 1.9 ± 0.4 nm (Fig. 1 b). High-resolution lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm can be observed from the high-resolution TEM image of 75E-CPDs, which is close to the (100) diffraction plane of graphene carbon (Fig. 1 c). The elemental composition and structure of 75E-CPDs were analyzed by XPS in Fig. 1 d, showing three peaks at 284.1, 402.1, and 532.1 eV, which belong to carbon (C, 57.52%), nitrogen (N, 2.71%), and oxygen (O, 39.77%), respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of 75E-CPDs indicates the existence of four types of characteristic peaks, including CC/CC (284.8 eV), CN (285.8 eV), CO (287.3 eV), and CO (289.9 eV) (Fig. 1 e). The prepared 75E-CPDs contain more content of CO than previously reported nitrogen-doped carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [18], indicating 75E-CPDs may be negatively charged. The high-resolution N1s spectrum of 75E-CPDs displays three characteristic peaks at 400.4 eV, 402 eV, and 405.5 eV, representing pyridine N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 1 f). The surface functional groups of 75E-CPDs were characterized by FTIR spectrum in Fig. 1 g. The peak at 3369 cm−1 represents free NH2, and the peak at 3119 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of =CH on the surface of benzene ring. The characteristic peaks at 2923 and 2854 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrational absorption of OH in the benzene ring. The peak at 1706 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of CO in the carboxyl group. The peak at 1607 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of the CC double bond in aromatics. The characteristic peaks at 1321 and 1040 cm−1 indicate the stretching vibrations of CN and CO, respectively. Finally, the peak at 802 cm−1 represents the characteristic peak of the out-of-plane bending vibration of CH in the benzene ring [39]. Notably, other E-CPDs also showed similar characteristic peaks (COOH, OH, and NH2), but the intensities of these characteristic peaks were different (Fig. S1). The intensity of the amino characteristic peak was the highest in 2E-CPDs, due to the high content of oPD in the raw materials. With the increase of EGCG content, the intensity of the amino characteristic peak gradually decreased, while the intensities of the carboxyl and hydroxyl characteristic peaks gradually increased, suggesting that E-CPDs contained the same groups, including carboxyl and phenolic hydroxyl groups, on their surfaces, but 75E-CPDs had the highest content of free functional groups. The ζ-potential of 75E-CPDs was -17.5 mV, consistent with the results of XPS and FTIR in which 75E-CPDs contained a high content of carboxyl and phenolic hydroxyl groups. It is considered that during the high-temperature carbonization process, carboxyl could be formed via the hydrolysis and cleavage of ester bonds in EGCG, but the phenolic hydroxyl groups of EGCG were retained [40,41]. Meanwhile, E-CPDs were carbonized during the hydrothermal process, resulting in high hydrophobicity. The negatively charged 75E-CPDs would bind to positively charged amino acid residues/cation clusters (His/HHQK) of Aβ by electrostatic interactions, thus suppressing the self-assembly of Aβ monomers [21,32,42-44]. The UV-Vis absorption and excitation/emission spectra of 75E-CPDs are exhibited in Fig. 1 h and Fig. S2. Due to the n-π* transition of CO bond, the absorption peak around 370 nm was observed, and the fluorescence emission signal at 450 nm upon the excitation at 370 nm was detected. Taken together, the above results demonstrated that the outer boundary of 75E-CPDs consisted of OH, COOH, and NH2, but the inner part was composed of benzene ring, pyridine ring, pyrrole ring, and CO bond, which were generated during the hydrothermal process. It is thus concluded that several functional groups of the raw material EGCG were retained in 75E-CPDs. They are expected to work effectively in inhibiting Aβ fibrillization as they do in EGCG [45,46].

CDs are a kind of fluorescent nanomaterials that can be used as imaging agents for the diagnosis of major diseases [19,47]. Therefore, the fluorescent properties of different E-CPDs (Table S1) that were synthesized by varying the ratio of EGCG:oPD were firstly investigated by a 3D excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectroscopy (3D EEMS). As shown in Fig. 1 i, a maximum emission at 450 nm upon exciting at 370 nm was observed for 75E-CPDs. The blue fluorescence of 75E-CPDs indicated their potential as fluorescent probes. 2E-CPDs exhibited a red fluorescence at 600 nm (Fig. S3a), while 75 mM solo EGCG-derived carbon dots (75E-CDs) exhibited a blue fluorescence at 400 nm (Fig. S3g). As illustrated in Fig. S3b-3f, with the increase of EGCG content from 2 to 5, 10, 50, 75, 100, or 112.5 mM, the fluorescence at 600 nm gradually disappeared, and the emission wavelength of E-CPDs gradually shifted from 600 nm to 450 nm, demonstrating the EGCG content affected the emission fluorescence of E-CPDs. Two causes might be responsible for this phenomenon: Firstly, with the increase of EGCG content, the carbonization degree of the prepared E-CPDs increased, leading to the reduction of the π-electron system and the increase of the energy gap of the surface states [47,48]. Secondly, high EGCG content could cause a relative decrease of nitrogen content, and an increase of the electron-withdrawing groups (COOH and CO) on the aromatic ring in E-CPDs, resulting in the fluorescence attenuation or quench [49,50]. The above 3D EEMS results demonstrated that the fluorescent properties of E-CPDs could be elegantly modulated by adjusting the composition content, providing the possibility for E-CPDs as imaging agents.

The effects of different E-CPDs on inhibiting Aβ40 fibrillization were firstly explored by in situ ThT fluorescence experiments. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the kinetics of Aβ40 aggregation showed a sigmoidal curve. The inhibitory efficiency of E-CPDs was enhanced with the increase of EGCG content, and 75 mM of EGCG gave E-CPDs the strongest inhibitory capability, while 2E-CPDs, 5E-CPDs, and 10E-CPDs were less effective. With the increase of EGCG content, the inhibitory effects of 50E-CPDs, 100E-CPDs, and 112.5E-CPDs were significantly enhanced, but the effects were weaker at low concentrations (below 20 µg/mL). As exhibited in Fig. 2
a, 75E-CPDs inhibited Aβ40 aggregation in a concentration-dependent manner. Notably, 75E-CPDs could completely inhibit the aggregation of Aβ40 at 80 µg/mL, and inhibit about 80% of ThT fluorescence intensity at 2.5 µg/mL, showing the strongest inhibitory effect than any other E-CPDs. It is considered that the catechol structure on the surface of E-CPDs also increased with increasing EGCG content in the raw materials (Fig. 1 d- 1 g and S1), leading to the enhanced inhibition capability. When the EGCG content was higher than 75 mM, the supersaturated catechol structure on the synthesized E-CPDs would undergo intramolecular hydrogen bonding, thereby weakening the interactions with Aβ species, and compromising inhibitory efficiency. The lag time of different E-CPDs was calculated and is listed in Fig. S5, demonstrating a similar trend. 75E-CPDs shortened the lag time of Aβ40 from 20 h to 9 h at 40 µg/mL, and to 3 h at 5 µg/mL, indicating that 75E-CPDs could promote the nucleation of Aβ40, which is consistent with the effect of EGCG. All other E-CPDs prolonged the Aβ40 lag phase. The inhibitory capability of 75E-CPDs was then compared with 75E-CDs and EGCG in Fig. S4g and S4h. At 2.5 µg/mL, 75E-CPDs, 75E-CDs, and EGCG inhibited 75%, 50%, and 40% of the ThT fluorescence intensity, respectively, indicating that 75E-CPDs possessed the highest inhibitory potency among the three agents.

Previous studies have indicated that polyphenols would affect the binding of ThT to amyloid aggregates [35,51,52]. Therefore, the above ThT results were further confirmed by ANS fluorescence and CR binding assays (Fig. S6). Due to the formation of a large number of hydrophobic patches during fibrillization, a remarkable ANS fluorescence ranging from 450 to 550 nm was observed in the Aβ40-only sample (Fig. S6a). With increasing the 75E-CPDs concentration from 2.5 to 80 µg/mL, the fluorescence intensity significantly decreased from 87% to 33%. Furthermore, the absorption spectra of CR confirmed the inhibition capability of 75E-CPDs (Fig. S6b). After 5 d of incubation, the remarkable CR absorbance indicated the formation of amyloid fibrils. In contrast, after co-incubation of Aβ40 solution with 75E-CPDs, the absorption intensities of CR decreased by 17% and 71% with 2.5 and 80 µg/mL of 75E-CPDs, respectively. The above results of ANS and CR experiments were consistent with the results of ThT experiments, further demonstrating that 75E-CPDs had the potent inhibitory capability and could effectively inhibit the formation of Aβ40 fibrils.

The feasibility of E-CPDs to inhibit Aβ40 fibrillization was also investigated by AFM imaging in Fig. 2 b and Fig. S7. Aβ40 alone aggregated into densely tangled fibrils, similar to previous reports [18,53]. With increasing 75E-CPDs concentration, the Aβ fibrils became shorter, and then formed amorphous aggregates, which subsequently transformed into small spherical and irregular aggregates, and ultimately almost disappeared. 75E-CPDs showed higher inhibitory capability than the other E-CPDs. To give a quantitative analysis of AFM images, the surface coverage of Aβ40 fibrils with different E-CPDs (80 µg/mL) in Fig. 2 b and Fig. S7 was then measured by Image J and is summarized in Fig. 2 c. The surface coverage of Aβ40 fibrils alone was 43%, and it decreased to 23% with 2E-CPDs, and to 11% with 75E-CPDs, indicating the potent inhibitory capability of 75E-CPDs. Notably, since 75E-CPDs are hydrophobic, Aβ40 aggregates and 75E-CPDs may compete with each other to bind to mica. Therefore, another control test without Aβ40 was performed to verify the accuracy of the AFM results. As can be seen from Fig. S8, 75E-CPDs could not be observed in AFM imaging when Aβ40 was not added, which excluded the influence of 75E-CPDs on the observation of Aβ40 fibrils. Moreover, the inhibitory capability of 75E-CPDs on Aβ40 fibrils formation was further confirmed by TEM experiments. As shown in Fig. S9, slender and dense amyloid fibrils were observed after 5 d of incubation of Aβ40 alone. With increasing 75E-CPDs concentration, the Aβ fibrils formed gradually became shorter and less. The formation of Aβ40 fibrils was almost completely inhibited by 80 µg/mL of 75E-CPDs. Taken together, AFM and TEM experiments further indicated that 75E-CPDs could effectively inhibit the formation of fibrillar Aβ40 aggregates.

Subsequently, the conformational transition of Aβ40 was investigated by circular dichroism spectroscopy. The addition of 75E-CPDs (2.5–80 µg/mL) to freshly prepared Aβ40 monomers did not change the negative peak around 200 nm (Fig. S10a), indicating 75E-CPDs had no obvious influence on the circular dichroism spectra of Aβ40. After 72 h of incubation, Aβ40 showed a negative peak around 218 nm, and a positive peak before 200 nm, demonstrating the formation of typical β-sheet structures. The negative peak intensity around 218 nm gradually decreased with increasing 75E-CPDs concentration (Fig. 2 d), demonstrating that 75E-CPDs could inhibit the conversion of Aβ40 from α-helix to β-sheet-rich structures. After incubation with 40–80 µg/mL 75E-CPDs for 72 h, Aβ40 did not show the typical characteristic peak of β-sheet structure, indicating that the aggregation pathway of Aβ40 had been changed. Comparatively, the influence of 75E-CDs on Aβ40 conformational transition has been investigated in Fig. S10b, and the quantitative analysis is illustrated in Fig. S11. At 80 µg/mL, 75E-CPDs reduced the β-sheet content from 43% to 28%, while 75E-CDs reduced the β-sheet content to 33%. These data suggested that 75E-CPDs inhibited the secondary structure transition of Aβ40 more strongly.

MTT assays with SH-SY5Y cells were conducted to evaluate the detoxification of 75E-CPDs. As shown in Fig. 2 e, within the tested concentration range (5–80 µg/mL), 75E-CPDs showed no obvious cytotoxicity, maintaining more than 95% cell viability. After treating SH-SY5Y cells with pre-incubated Aβ40 for 24 h, the cell viability dropped to 53% (Fig. 2 f). 75E-CPDs dose-dependently attenuated the toxic effect of Aβ40, increasing the cell viability to more than 80% at 80 µg/mL. Then the FDA/PI double staining assay was conducted to validate the detoxification of 75E-CPDs. As shown in Fig. 2 g, Aβ40 aggregates could lead to many PI-positive cells, while 75E-CPDs reduced the number of red fluorescent cells, indicating that 75E-CPDs attenuated Aβ40-induced neuronal death.

Previous reports have demonstrated that the catechol structure of EGCG can function on inhibiting the aggregation of other amyloid proteins [30]. For broadening the applications of 75E-CPDs, the effects of 75E-CPDs on suppressing Aβ42 and human islet amyloid polypeptide (hIAPP) fibrillization were investigated. ThT kinetic experiments showed that 75E-CPDs also had a strong inhibitory effect on the aggregation of Aβ42 (Fig. S12a). At 2.5 and 20 µg/mL, 75E-CPDs inhibited 25% and 85% of Aβ42 fibrils, respectively. At 80 µg/mL 75E-CPDs, the fibrillization was almost completely inhibited. The results of AFM experiments further confirmed the strong inhibitory capability of 75E-CPDs on Aβ42 fibrillization (Fig. S12b). Similarly, as shown in Fig. S12c, 75E-CPDs could inhibit hIAPP aggregation in a concentration-dependent manner, decreasing 60% of hIAPP ThT fluorescence intensity at 2.5 µg/mL. Therefore, 75E-CPDs are also a potent inhibitor for suppression of hIAPP aggregation. In conclusion, we confirmed that 75E-CPDs could potently suppress the fibrillization of amyloid proteins like Aβ40, Aβ42, and hIAPP, and it is considered to function on many other amyloids.

The superiority of prepared 75E-CPDs was further validated by comparing it with the currently available CDs in Table S2. It can be seen that the addition of EGCG improved the inhibitory capability of CDs, which may be due to the retention of the effective functional group (catechol structure) of the precursor EGCG [19,27,54]. Moreover, the functional groups on the surface of E-CPDs, including carboxyl, phenolic hydroxyl, and amino groups, can bind with Aβ species via various interactions, thus maintaining the state of Aβ species and interfering with the formation of Aβ fibrils. Therefore, with further prolongation of the incubation time, the ThT fluorescence intensity kept unchanged. Taken together, 75E-CPDs showed the strongest inhibitory effects than other CDs (Fig. S13 and Table S2). It is worth noting that the inhibition effect of E-CPDs was higher than CPDs, and the fluorescence characteristics of some E-CPDs, including 2E-CPDs, 5E-CPDs, and 10E-CPDs, were comparable with CPDs. In detail, 2E-CPDs, 5E-CPDs, and 10E-CPDs are effective scavengers with red fluorescence, while 50E-CPDs, 75E-CPDs, 100E-CPDs, and 112.5E-CPDs can emit blue fluorescence.

From above results, it concluded that the most potent CDs, 75E-CPDs, can be obtained by using 75 mM EGCG as one of the raw materials. 75E-CPDs could inhibit about 80% of ThT fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2 a), change the Aβ40 fibrils into small spherical and amorphous aggregates (Fig. 2 b), reduce the β-sheet content of Aβ from 43% to 28% (Fig. 2 d and S11), and rescue cell viability from 53% to 80% (Fig. 2 f). Therefore, 75E-CPDs are a potent scavenger against Aβ fibrillization. The multifunctionality of 75E-CPDs involved in the pathological process of AD will be further explored in the following assays, which is beneficial for AD theranostics.

The destruction or remodeling of existing Aβ fibrils is an important therapeutic strategy for AD treatment [55,56], so the dissociation capability of 75E-CPDs was evaluated by ThT fluorescence and AFM imaging assays. Within 4 h, 5 µg/mL of 75E-CDs and EGCG reduced the Aβ40 ThT fluorescence intensity by about 10% and 30%, respectively (Fig. S14a-14b). Besides, it was demonstrated that the disaggregation effect of 75E-CDs decreased rapidly at low concentrations, and EGCG could not complete the disaggregation of Aβ40 fibrils within a short time of 4 h. In contrast, 5 µg/mL of 75E-CPDs reduced the ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ40 fibrils by 40% (Fig. 3
a). It can be intuitively seen in Fig. 3 b that 75E-CPDs had a stronger disaggregation effect than EGCG and 75E-CDs, and still retained high disaggregation capability at low concentrations. To the best of our knowledge, there are few reports of scavengers with such rapid Aβ disaggregation capability, whether small molecules, proteins, or nanomaterials [18,57-59]. Compared with the scavengers in literatures, 75E-CPDs significantly decomposed long and dense Aβ fibrils into short fragments within 5 min, showing a highly efficient capability to disrupt mature Aβ fibrils on a time scale of seconds to minutes (Fig. 3 a, 3 e, and S15). The functional groups of 75E-CPDs, including carboxyl, phenolic hydroxyl, amino, and benzene rings, are considered to interact with Aβ fibrils via hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and hydrophobic interactions, which resulted in the breaking of intermolecular Aβ salt bridges and the disruption of the highly ordered fibrillar structures [58,60,61]. In addition, it can be seen from Fig. S14c-S14d that the Aβ species maintained the disrupted state even when extending the incubation time to 20 h. Because the multiple interactions between 75E-CPDs and Aβ species would keep them in the bound state, the disrupted fragments were unable to reaggregate.

Due to the existence of mass of biomolecules in vivo environment, the disaggregation activity of 75E-CPDs may be influenced by those biomolecules. Therefore, BSA was selected as a model molecule to explore the disaggregation capability of 75E-CPDs in the presence of in vivo biomolecules. As shown in Fig. S14c, the effect of 25 µM BSA on 75E-CPDs was negligible, and 75E-CPDs still maintained a rapid disaggregation capability. Then, the effect of BSA concentration on the disaggregation activity of 80 µg/mL 75E-CPDs was explored. In the presence of 100 µM BSA, 75E-CPDs still maintained a rapid disaggregation capability (Fig. S14d). This result demonstrated that 75E-CPDs might effectively work in vivo on disaggregating amyloids in the presence of other biomolecules.

Then, the rapid disaggregation of mature Aβ40 fibrils by 75E-CPDs was observed via AFM imaging. As exhibited in Fig. 3 c, Aβ40 fibrils maintained long and dense fibrillar morphology after 4 h coincubation. With increasing the concentration of 75E-CPDs from 2.5 to 20 µg/mL, Aβ40 fibrils became short and broken, and almost no aggregates could be observed at 40 and 80 µg/mL, indicating 75E-CPDs could significantly disrupt Aβ40 fibrils. Furthermore, the AFM images of Aβ40 fibrils at different time intervals were recorded. As displayed in Fig. S15, the degradation of Aβ40 fibrils by 75E-CPDs could be observed within 5 min. Moreover, the fibrils gradually disintegrated within 4 h, visual evidence that 75E-CPDs depolymerized the amyloids. By contrast, EGCG showed weak disaggregation capability even at 40 and 80 µg/mL, and there existed visible long fibrils (Fig. S16). Additionally, the disaggregation capability of 75E-CPDs and 75E-CDs was observed under an inverted fluorescence microscope (Fig. 3 d and Fig. S17). Aβ40 cultured alone formed a large number of fluorescent plaques. After coincubation with 75E-CPDs and 75E-CDs for 4 h, the number of Aβ40 fibrils decreased in a concentration-dependent manner. Notably, 75E-CPDs had a more potent disaggregation effect at low concentrations (below 5 µg/mL). The above results were consistent with the ThT kinetic results, proving that 75E-CPDs possessed potent depolymerization capability.

The above ThT and AFM results indicate that 75E-CPDs could disaggregate Aβ fibrils quite rapidly. To understand more details of the disaggregation, stopped-flow fluorescence spectroscopy was conducted to investigate the fast disaggregation kinetics of Aβ40 fibrils. As shown in Fig. 3 e, 1, 2, 5, and 10 µg/mL of 75E-CPDs efficiently disaggregated 2%, 4%, 11%, and 16% of Aβ fibrils within 50 s, respectively, indicating that 75E-CPDs significantly destroyed Aβ40 fibrils within second-time scale. It is speculated that using EGCG as one of the raw materials enriched the 75E-CPDs surface with benzene rings, carboxyl, and phenolic hydroxyl groups, leading to the enhanced interactions with Aβ40 fibrils, which plays critical roles in the rapid disaggregation of Aβ40 fibrils.

At present, the occurrence of AD is mainly assessed by the memory status of patients, when the patients are already in the advanced stage of AD [62]. Determination of the deposition level of biomarker Aβ is a promising and effective method for the early diagnosis of AD [63]. The feasibility of using fluorescent 75E-CPDs as probes to monitor the amyloid fibrillization in the progress of AD was investigated by fluorescence spectrophotometry and inverted fluorescence microscopy. The fluorescent responsiveness of 75E-CPDs to Aβ fibrils was firstly verified. Free 75E-CPDs (50 µg/mL) showed weak fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4
a), while the fluorescence intensity increased upon mixing with Aβ40 fibrils, and reached 10-fold higher at 5 µM Aβ40 fibrils (Fig. 4 b). The multiple interactions between 75E-CPDs and Aβ aggregates led to a restricted intramolecular movement, resulting in enhanced fluorescence intensity [64,65]. On the other hand, after interacting with Aβ plaques, the aggregated 75E-CPDs were distributed on the plaque surface, resulting in a fluorescent “turn-on” phenomenon [66]. To further elucidate the potential of 75E-CPDs to monitor Aβ40 fibrils, fluorescence images of 75E-CPDs after coincubation with Aβ40 fibrils were recorded and analyzed. As shown in Fig. 4 c (1), a large amount of green fluorescent spots of ThT-Aβ40 aggregates were observed after incubation for 72 h, indicating the formation of Aβ fibrils. Due to the inhibitory effects of 75E-CPDs, the green fluorescence signal of ThT-labeled Aβ40 aggregates gradually vanished with increasing 75E-CPDs concentration, which was consistent with the inhibition results (Fig. 2). Notably, distinct blue fluorescence was observed under UV channel (Fig. 4 c (7)-(10)), and the distribution was consistent with that of green ThT fluorescence in Fig. 4 c (2)-(5). These fluorescence images indicated that both 75E-CPDs and ThT fluorescence could label Aβ40 aggregates, providing the basis for localization and diagnosis of Aβ aggregates. As for ThT, when it binds to amyloid fibrils, the fluorescence is enhanced. However, it possesses neither inhibitory nor disaggregation effects on amyloidosis [67]. The above results showed that 75E-CPDs could detect and clear Aβ40 fibrils, acting as an “indicator” and “scavenger” of Aβ plaques, which suggested the application potential of 75E-CPDs in AD theranostics.

Literatures have shown that the catechol structure works as an antioxidant group [6,30], so the retained catechol structure may endow 75E-CPDs with ROS scavenging activity. Intracellular ROS scavenging activity of 75E-CPDs was thus evaluated with SH-SY5Y cells by DCFH-DA assay. As demonstrated in Fig. 5
a, the SH-SY5Y cells showed obvious green fluorescence after coincubation with Aβ40, and the cell morphology significantly changed, indicating a large amount of ROS was generated and the cell structure was destroyed. The addition of 75E-CPDs reduced the green fluorescence intensity and restored cell morphology, manifesting that 75E-CPDs could scavenge intracellular ROS and alleviate the oxidative damage of ROS to cells (Fig. 5 b). Taken together, 75E-CPDs have dual effects at the cellular level, that is, they can not only inhibit Aβ40-induced cytotoxicity, but also scavenge ROS caused by Aβ40 aggregation, thereby exerting a synergistic effect to increase the cell viability and restore the cell morphology.

Since C. elegans mutant CL2006 expresses Aβ42 in the body wall muscle [68–70], C. elegans were used as an in vivo model to evaluate the capabilities of 75E-CPDs for scavenging and imaging of Aβ deposits in the nematodes. As shown in Fig. 6 , after staining with ThT and 75E-CPDs, evident ThT-labeled Aβ42 plaques (green fluorescent spots in Fig. 6 a) and 75E-CPDs-labeled Aβ42 plaques (blue fluorescent spots in Fig. 6 b) could be seen in CL2006 nematodes. Conversely, no such fluorescent spots were observed in N2 nematodes due to the absence of Aβ42 in vivo (Fig. 6 c and 6 d). This result demonstrated the potential of 75E-CPDs for in vivo detection of Aβ plaques.

The amyloid inhibition capability was then explored by ThT staining assay. As a control, no amyloid plaque was observed in wild-type N2 nematodes (Fig. 6 e). After staining CL2006 nematodes with ThT dyes, evident green fluorescent spots were observed (Fig. 6 f), while no obvious fluorescent plaques could be seen for CL2006 nematodes administrated with 75E-CPDs (Fig. 6 g and 6 h). These results indicated that 75E-CPDs could clear Aβ42 plaques in CL2006 nematodes. In addition, obvious blue fluorescence of 75E-CPDs could be seen in the administered CL2006, indicating that 75E-CPDs mainly accumulated in the intestinal lumen of the nematodes (Fig. 6 i).

The benefits of 75E-CPDs on C. elegans lifespan were then explored. 75E-CPDs had no influence on the lifespan of wild-type N2 nematodes (Fig. 6 j), indicating their good biocompatibility. The lifespan of C. elegans mutant CL2006 nematodes was about 14 d. 75E-CDs, EGCG, and CPDs (80 µg/mL) prolonged the lifespan of CL2006 nematodes by 5 d, 5 d, and 4 d, respectively. Notably, 75E-CPDs (80 µg/mL) extended the lifespan of CL2006 nematodes for 6 d, suggesting 75E-CPDs had the best therapeutic effect. Additionally, the mortality of CL2006 nematodes was reduced from the initial stage of culture, demonstrating that 75E-CPDs could reduce the paralysis rate of CL2006 nematodes, clear the movement disorder of CL2006 nematodes, and prolong the lifespan of CL2006 nematodes. These results suggested that 75E-CPDs could be used as an effective scavenger to mitigate Aβ deposition in vivo, further demonstrating the potential of 75E-CPDs in the treatment of AD.

To better understand the protective effects of 75E-CPDs, the egg-laying behavior of the nematodes was analyzed. Fig. 6 k shows that 75E-CPDs could improve the number of eggs laid per day of CL2006 nematodes from the initial stage. Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. 6 l, the addition of 75E-CPDs had little effect on the number of eggs laid by N2 nematodes. In detail, the total number of eggs laid by the CL2006 nematodes was approximately 38% less than that by the N2 nematodes, but the 75E-CPDs treatment increased the number of CL2006 eggs laid by approximately 35%. This enhancement was higher than that reported previously with 3,6’-disinapoyl sucrose [36]. These results suggested that 75E-CPDs had effective protection of the CL2006 nematodes from the amyloid toxicity.

However, it should be noted that there are several limitations of C. elegans as an AD model [71]. In C. elegans, some AD-related genes such as β-secretase are lacking, which prevents C. elegans from endogenously producing β-amyloid peptides. Besides, many mammalian features, such as circulatory system, myelinated neurons, hippocampus, and adaptive immune system, are absent in C. elegans. Therefore, further tests by other model validation are necessary before considering clinical trials.

In this work, a series of EGCG-derived carbonized polymer dots (E-CPDs) were fabricated by using EGCG as one of the raw materials, and the multifunctionality of E-CPDs targeting amyloid deposition, including inhibition of Aβ fibrillation, disaggregation of Aβ fibrils, amyloid detection, intracellular ROS scavenging, and lifespan extension of AD model nematodes, was explored. Among the prepared E-CPDs and 75E-CDs, 75E-CPDs that were synthesized from 75 mM EGCG and 50 mM oPD showed the most potent inhibitory and disaggregation capabilities. 75E-CPDs could effectively inhibit the formation of β-sheet-rich Aβ aggregates, rapidly disaggregate mature Aβ40 fibrils, detect the plaques formed by Aβ aggregation, scavenge Aβ-induced intracellular ROS, and prolong the lifespan of CL2006 nematodes from 14 d to 20 d. The active groups including benzene ring and phenolic hydroxyl group from the raw material would interact with Aβ species via hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, and hydrophobic interactions, leading to the change of Aβ aggregation pathway. Future studies should direct toward detailed in vivo experiments to test the clinical applicability of this scavenger. The innovative idea of designing CDs with a small molecular Aβ inhibitor has extended the applicability of functional CDs in AD theranostics, and the research has provided important insights into the development of multifunctional carbon nanomaterials for fighting against neurodegenerative diseases and other protein conformational diseases.
